title: Incident Response Standard Operating Procedure
description: Comprehensive incident response procedure covering severity classification (SEV1-4), full incident lifecycle (detection, triage, investigation, mitigation, resolution, post-mortem), communication templates, and on-call expectations.
anti_patterns: no defined severity levels leading to inconsistent response
skipping incident documentation and real-time note-taking
attempting root cause fix before mitigating user impact
no escalation path when on-call cannot resolve within timeframe
single-person incident response without team coordination
not communicating with stakeholders during active incidents
ignoring low-severity incidents that may indicate systemic issues
implementation_steps: Classify incident severity: SEV1 Critical (complete outage or data loss affecting >25% users, all hands, 30-min mitigation target), SEV2 Major (10-25% users or core feature unavailable, on-call + team lead, 2-hour mitigation target), SEV3 Minor (<10% users or non-core feature degraded, on-call during business hours, 8-hour target), SEV4 Low (cosmetic or within SLO, next sprint)

Execute Phase 1 - Detection: receive alerts from monitoring (Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog via PagerDuty), user reports escalated by support (severity assessment matrix), or engineer observation; create incident record with description, severity, affected services, detection source, and timestamp

Execute Phase 2 - Triage: assign incident commander based on severity (SEV1: engineering lead or designated IC, SEV2: team lead of affected service, SEV3/4: on-call engineer); IC confirms incident is real, assigns severity, opens dedicated Slack channel (#inc-YYYYMMDD-brief-description), assembles response team by paging on-call engineers, initiates stakeholder communication

Execute Phase 3 - Investigation: (1) Check recent deployments in last 4 hours for correlation and evaluate rollback, (2) Review error rates, latency, CPU/memory, and application logs around incident start time, (3) Trace distributed requests using Jaeger/Zipkin to identify failure points, (4) Verify health of external dependencies (databases, caches, third-party APIs) for cascading failures, (5) Reproduce in staging if possible to confirm root cause hypothesis; document all findings in incident channel in real-time

Execute Phase 4 - Mitigation: apply fastest strategy to restore service—Rollback (revert to previous version), Feature Flag Disable (turn off problematic feature without redeploying), Traffic Rerouting (shift away from affected region/instance), Scaling (increase instances for capacity exhaustion), Circuit Breaker (shed load on failing dependency, serve degraded responses), Database Failover (promote read replica); IC authorizes action, engineer executes, stakeholders updated once mitigation confirmed

Execute Phase 5 - Resolution: develop and test fix in dev environment → deploy to staging and validate → follow standard deployment SOP for production → remove temporary mitigations (re-enable feature flags, reset circuit breakers, reduce scaled instances) → monitor for 24 hours to confirm stability and no regressions

Execute Phase 6 - Post-Mortem: conduct blameless post-mortem within 48 hours for SEV1/SEV2, within one week for SEV3; document incident summary (timeline from detection to resolution), root cause analysis (technical root cause and contributing factors), impact assessment (duration, affected users, data loss), what went well, what could be improved, and specific/assigned/time-bound action items; track action items in project management system with weekly review until completion

Follow on-call expectations: primary on-call reachable within 5 minutes, start investigation within 15 minutes; weekly rotations with handoff at 09:00 UTC Mondays; on-call must have access to all production systems, monitoring dashboards, and incident management tool; escalate to secondary on-call and engineering lead if SEV1 not resolved within 30 minutes
rules: SEV1 (Critical): complete outage or data loss affecting >25% users; all hands response; 30-minute mitigation target
SEV2 (Major): significant degradation affecting 10-25% users or core feature unavailable; on-call + team lead; 2-hour mitigation target
SEV3 (Minor): limited impact affecting <10% users or non-core feature degraded; on-call during business hours; 8-hour mitigation target
SEV4 (Low): cosmetic or within SLO thresholds; logged and addressed in next sprint
Incident commander assignment: SEV1 = engineering lead or designated IC, SEV2 = team lead of affected service, SEV3/4 = on-call engineer
IC must confirm incident is real, assign severity, open dedicated Slack channel, assemble response team, initiate stakeholder communication
Investigation must check recent deployments first (last 4 hours) and evaluate rollback if correlation found
All investigation findings must be documented in the incident channel in real-time for team members joining later
Mitigation aims to restore service ASAP even if root cause is not fully understood; approved strategies: rollback, feature flag disable, traffic rerouting, scaling, circuit breaker, database failover
IC authorizes mitigation action; responsible engineer executes; stakeholders updated once mitigation confirmed effective
After mitigation, follow standard deployment SOP for permanent fix; remove all temporary mitigations; monitor for 24 hours
Blameless post-mortem required within 48 hours for SEV1/SEV2, within one week for SEV3
Post-mortem must include: incident summary timeline, root cause analysis, impact assessment, what went well, what could improve, and assigned time-bound action items
Action items tracked in project management system and reviewed weekly until completion
Primary on-call must be reachable within 5 minutes and start investigation within 15 minutes
On-call rotations are weekly with handoff at 09:00 UTC on Mondays
On-call must have access to all production systems, monitoring dashboards, and incident management tool
Escalate to secondary on-call and engineering lead if SEV1 not resolved within 30 minutes

examples.name: SEV1 Incident: Complete Service Outage
examples.description: Trading service returns 503 for all requests affecting >25% of active users → PagerDuty alerts on-call engineer → engineering lead assigned as IC → #inc-20260305-trading-outage opened → investigation reveals failed database connection pool → circuit breaker tripped to shed load → root cause: connection pool exhaustion from unbounded query → mitigation: scale database replicas and restart connection pool → resolution: add connection pool limits in code → post-mortem within 48 hours

examples.name: Communication Template - Initial
examples.description: 'We are investigating reports of [brief description]. The incident has been classified as SEV[1-4]. The response team is actively working on mitigation. Next update in [15/30/60] minutes.'

examples.name: Communication Template - Mitigated
examples.description: 'The incident affecting [brief description] has been mitigated as of [timestamp]. Services are recovering. We are monitoring for stability and working on a permanent fix.'

examples.name: Communication Template - Resolved
examples.description: 'The incident affecting [brief description] has been fully resolved as of [timestamp]. A post-mortem will be published within [48 hours/1 week].'

examples.name: Mitigation via Feature Flag Disable
examples.description: New feature causes increased error rate → IC authorizes feature flag disable → on-call engineer turns off the flag via feature flag service without redeploying → error rate returns to baseline → stakeholders notified of mitigation → team develops fix → follows standard deployment SOP for permanent resolution
